data.title: Growing Singapore demand boosts Malaysian bus firm’s IPO
data.description:
KUALA LUMPUR - HI Mobility is betting on increased traffic with Singapore to drive growth as the Malaysian operator of Causeway Link buses readies to start trading on March 28 after a rare listing by one of the country’s transport providers. 

The company, which is the biggest cross-border bus operator in both countries, has raised RM116 million (S$35 million) in an initial public offering (IPO) that values the firm at US$137 million (S$183.5 million).   

Chief executive officer Lim Chern Chuen is seeing a change in the profile of his customers and strong growth in its cross-border bus services between Malaysia’s Johor state and Singapore over the last few years. 

While bus services were traditionally used by Malaysians going to work in Singapore, more people are travelling in the other direction to Johor, following the creation of a special economic zone, Mr Lim said in an interview. More than 300,000 people cross the border daily, making it one of the busiest land crossings in the world. 

HI Mobility’s revenue rose 73 per cent to RM207.7 million in 2024 from the previous year, while gross profit more than doubled. It operates 155 Causeway Link buses on the Singapore-Johor route, around a quarter of its overall fleet.

Nearly half its revenue in 2024 came from Singapore. It also operates bus services in the Malaysian capital Kuala Lumpur and the states of Selangor and Melaka. 

The company’s stock market debut is a rare success story in Malaysia’s public transport landscape, which is mostly dominated by closely-held state-linked firms. 

Mr Lim said that the company’s success in gaining profitability is in part due to its pursuit of automation to increase efficiency, and it is now also offering automation and technological solutions as a service to other operators.

“If we look at mobility as a whole, that is where the market becomes even bigger, beyond just the buses we operate,” he said.

Founded in 2002 as Causeway Link by Mr Lim’s father Lim Han Weng, the firm was the first Malaysian bus operator to win a  cross-border service licence. The elder Lim also founded Johor-based logistics firm Yinson Holdings which has now transformed into an energy infrastructure firm with a market value of US$1.4 billion. Yinson is currently planning an IPO for its offshore floating production unit in the United States. 

HI Mobility’s IPO will make it the third-largest listing in Malaysia this year, where US$127 million has been raised so far – the most in South-east Asia. Malaysia’s stock market was also the region’s top IPO destination, hosting over US$1.8 billion worth of IPOs. BLOOMBERG

data.title: Top Fed official warns tariff-driven inflation boost is ‘inevitable’
data.description:
BOSTON - US Federal Reserve Bank of Boston President Susan Collins said it looks “inevitable” that tariffs will boost inflation, at least in the near term, adding it’s likely appropriate to keep interest rates steady for longer. 

While tariff-driven inflation could prove short-lived, Ms Collins said it’s possible price pressures could prove more persistent.

“It looks inevitable that tariffs are going to increase inflation in the near term,” Ms Collins said on March 27 during a fireside conversation in Boston. “My kind of modal outlook would be that that could be short-lived with a continuation of some disinflation, but further in the future than I might have expected before.”

“But there are risks around that, and depending on how things unfold, it may be more persistent and a larger increase,” she said.

Fed officials left interest rates unchanged on March 19 for a second straight meeting. Policymakers have said borrowing costs are well positioned to wait for greater clarity on the economic impact of President Donald Trump’s policy changes, including trade and immigration.

Chair Jerome Powell said last week that the economy remains strong and the labour market is solid despite elevated uncertainty. He also said that any tariff-induced inflation is likely to be transitory. St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem, however, said on March 26 that policymakers should be wary of assuming such inflation would be entirely temporary.

Ms Collins highlighted two channels to watch for the impact of tariffs on inflation. Those include import prices, which she said tend to reflect levies quickly, as well as the degree to which higher costs are passed along to consumers.  

The Boston Fed chief said Americans might respond differently to a pickup in price pressures than what’s being suggested by economic models given the recent period of high inflation.

Fed officials marked down their estimates for growth this year while lifting their bets for inflation, according to updated projections released after the central bank’s latest gathering. They also raised their estimates for unemployment, while noting there’s increased uncertainty for their forecasts. 

Ms Collins said it’s too early to predict the economic impact of tariffs, though she said a downturn in demand from worsening consumer sentiment is a “very plausible” scenario. Even so, she said the fundamentals of the economy remain robust. Currently, data point to a “wait-and-see” attitude among consumers, Collins said. 

Ms Collins highlighted the importance of inflation expectations, especially in a situation of more broad-based levies and retaliatory measures. 

“That’s something that I take very seriously, and we’ll have to watch the data and see how things unfold and make decisions accordingly,” she said. 

Several measures point to a pickup in consumers’ estimates for price increases in the near term, and a University of Michigan gauge of inflation expectations over the next five to 10 years has surged to the highest level in more than three decades. BLOOMBERG

data.title: Indonesia’s Widjaja family moves to delist Sinarmas Land from SGX; more large-cap exits on the horizon
data.description:
SINGAPORE - An entity controlled by Indonesia’s billionaire Widjaja family has made a voluntary unconditional cash offer for all the shares in Sinarmas Land that it does not already own at 31 cents apiece.

The offer on March 27 values the Singapore-listed property developer at $1.32 billion.

The offeror, Lyon Investments – which counts executive chairman Franky Oesman Widjaja, chief executive officer Muktar Widjaja and Margaretha Natalia Widjaja among its directors – currently holds about 70.3 per cent of the total number of issued shares in Sinarmas Land.

The offer price represents a 12.7 per cent premium over Sinarmas Land’s last traded price of 27.5 cents per share on March 24, before the company called for a trading halt on March 25 before the market opened.

It also represents a premium of about 17.1, 5.6 and 14.6 per cent over the volume weighted average price of the shares over the last one, three and 12-month periods, respectively.

However, the offer price is at a 73.9 per cent discount to Sinarmas Land’s net asset value of $1.19 per share as at Jun 30, 2024.

The offeror said the trading liquidity of the shares on the Singapore Exchange (SGX) in the past year has generally been thin, and that the offer allows shareholders to “realise a clean cash exit at a premium”.

It added that it was making the offer to delist the company from SGX’s mainboard.

This will give the management more flexibility to manage the business, optimise the use of its management and capital resources, and facilitate the implementation of any operational change without the attendant costs, regulatory restrictions and compliance issues associated with its listed status, it said.

Trading in shares of Sinarmas Land will be suspended should less than 10 per cent of its total number of shares be held in public hands at the end of the offer.

The offeror said that, in this event, it does not intend to support or take any step for the public float to be restored or for any trading suspension to be lifted.

It added that it intends to exercise its right of compulsory acquisition, should it manage to acquire at least 90 per cent of shares it did not already own before the offer.

The Widjaja family, which controls the Sinar Mas Group, is estimated to have a net worth of US$18.9 billion (S$25.3 billion) as at end-December, according to Forbes, ranking it as the fourth-richest in Indonesia.

The conglomerate has interests in a wide range of businesses, including paper, property and banking.

data.title: US stocks extend losses on auto tariff shock
data.description:
NEW YORK - Wall Street stocks retreated for a second day on March 27, as US President Donald Trump’s announcement of upcoming tariffs on imported vehicles and parts rocked financial markets and sparked global jitters.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell by 0.4 per cent to 42,299.70, while the broad-based S&P 500 Index lost 0.3 per cent to 5,693.31.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite Index tumbled 0.5 per cent to 17,804.03.

Analysts said the imposition of the tariffs – with further announcements on levies on the way on April 2 – had delivered a shock to global markets.

“I think there’s a lot of confusion right now,” said Mr Tom Cahill, of Ventura Wealth Management.

Investors have been expecting more details surrounding Mr Trump’s promised tariffs on April 2, with markets previously lifted by hopes that they would be more targeted than anticipated.

Mr Trump has vowed to impose reciprocal tariffs to address practices that his administration deems unfair, but the White House earlier signaled that separate sector-specific action might not come on the same day.

But Mr Trump’s announced 25 per cent auto tariffs, to take effect next week, came unexpectedly to investors, Mr Cahill added.

Around half of vehicles sold in the United States are imported, and the supply chain for North American automobile production involves parts crossing borders multiple times.

This means that tariffs would likely raise costs for consumers.

On March 27, shares in major automaker Ford declined 3.9 per cent, while General Motors plunged 7.4 per cent. Stellantis shares dropped by 1.2 per cent.

While markets have tried to rally, this effort “lacks conviction,” said Mr Cahill, as it is risky getting ahead of Trump’s April 2 announcement. AFP

data.title: When the average investors can’t ride out the market turmoil
data.description:
After the dot.com bubble burst in the early 2000s, Mr Lars Staack decided to play it safe and invest his retirement savings in S&P 500 index funds, which are diversified and carry lower risk than owning individual stocks.

It was a strategy that brought him peace of mind for more than two decades – until US President Donald Trump was elected in November 2024. As he reviewed Mr Trump’s comments in support of sweeping tariffs, Mr Staack, 62, who retired two years ago, became increasingly uneasy about the savings he planned to use for the rest of his retirement.

data.title: Avoid bad investment moves that even rich investors will make
data.description:
Decades as an investor and trader on Wall Street have taught me that market panics come and go. Geopolitics, inflation and profit warnings, among other variables, can make a mess of things â and the ensuing volatility often fuels our urge to make a decision, any decision. Especially the bad kind.Â 

How investors respond to market turmoil is, of course, what behavioral finance is all about. And, just like in baseball, what you want to avoid are unforced errors, which have a tendency to contribute to long-term financial harm. Thereâs an endless assortment of ways to make mistakes that hurt your portfolio, though most fall into four broad categories: believing things that are not true;Â attempting to operate outside of your skill set;Â allowing your behavior to be driven by emotions; and failing to let time work for you.

data.title: Budget-minded Gen Z shoppers fuel instant coffee boom
data.description:
What happens if it becomes increasingly costly to buy a freshly brewed cup of coffee at the cafe? You start making it yourself at home or at the office.

Young people looking to save money on their increasingly expensive morning caffeine habit are turning to a throwback: instant coffee.

data.title: Early adopter advantage: Get ahead of the curve with prime Marina South waterfront living
data.description:
Rising in the heart of Marina South, One Marina Gardens is poised to redefine urban living in one of Singapore’s most anticipated districts. The development, with its prime location and seamless connectivity, is set against the backdrop of the evolving Greater Southern Waterfront, offering residents a front-row seat to the city’s next chapter.

With panoramic views and a thoughtfully integrated design, One Marina Gardens reflects the changing landscape of waterfront living in Singapore.

One Marina Gardens boasts an enviable location in Marina South, Singapore’s newest prime district. Just minutes from the Central Business District (CBD), the development places residents within reach of the city’s financial and commercial hubs.

Public transport options are easily accessible, making it easy to travel around Singapore. Residents will enjoy direct access to the upcoming Marina South MRT Station on the Thomson-East Coast Line, ensuring effortless access to Shenton Way, Orchard Road and beyond.

Framed by some of Singapore’s most iconic landmarks, including Marina Bay Sands and Gardens by the Bay, the development is immersed in a setting where urban energy meets natural beauty. Residents will enjoy convenient access to a large hawker centre, Satay by the Bay, located inside Gardens by the Bay, offering an authentic local dining experience within walking distance.

Whether it is dining at world-class restaurants, taking in the greenery of the bayfront, or strolling along the waterfront promenade, life here unfolds against a backdrop of some of the city’s most celebrated destinations.

Elevated living, inside and out

One Marina Gardens offers a vantage point over the Straits of Singapore, delivering panoramic sea views and a sense of tranquillity rarely found in the heart of the city. As part of the Urban Redevelopment Authority’s (URA) Greater Southern Waterfront master plan, the district is set to evolve into a vibrant mixed-use enclave, where residential, commercial and leisure spaces come together to create a dynamic urban ecosystem.

As the first residential project at Marina Gardens Lane, the development signals the start of a new chapter for the precinct. With infrastructure enhancements and new amenities taking shape, early adopters can capitalise on the first-mover advantage to become part of a growing, future-ready neighbourhood – one set to become a highly sought-after address.

One Marina Gardens offers 937 thoughtfully designed units across two towers – 30 and 44 storeys tall – that accommodate a range of lifestyles. 

From cosy one-bedroom apartments suited for urban professionals to expansive four-bedroom premium units ideal for families, each home is crafted with a focus on natural light, ventilation and sweeping views – whether of the city skyline, lush landscaped grounds or the tranquil waterfront.

Beyond private living spaces, the development boasts a network of communal and wellness-focused facilities spread across various levels of the development. At the heart of it is a 50m pool on the landscaped deck, perfect for dedicated swimmers, while poolside cabanas provide a retreat for those looking to unwind.

For those drawn to panoramic vistas, Sky Terraces and Sky Gardens offer elevated experiences, featuring an outdoor gym, yoga terrace, family lounge and viewing decks that capture the vibrancy of the Marina Bay district. 

Back at ground level, a range of social spaces – including a pocket garden and restaurant – set the stage for gatherings with family and friends.

The development also includes a kids’ pool, playground, games room and an on-site childcare centre, ensuring a well-rounded environment for all generations. Meanwhile, retail spaces on the first level bring everyday conveniences to residents’ doorsteps, reinforcing a seamless, self-sufficient lifestyle.

The 10-minute walkable lifestyle

Designed for effortless movement, One Marina Gardens will be deeply integrated with its surroundings, forming a 10-minute neighbourhood that will provide residents both unparalleled convenience and an urban experience like no other. 

Located steps away from an elevated landscape bridge that will provide direct links to nearby destinations such as Gardens by the Bay and Marina Coastal Park, the development gives residents access to scenic experiences during their daily commutes and leisure strolls.

For all-weather accessibility, an underground pedestrian link will connect key areas within the neighbourhood, offering a practical solution for Singapore’s tropical climate. The neighbourhood will also feature approximately 5,000 sq m of commercial space with retail offerings on the first level at the second land parcel in Marina Garden, located opposite One Marina Gardens. This commercial hub will further enhance the self-contained nature of the district. Meanwhile, future open plazas will create spaces for social gatherings, events and community interaction, fostering a sense of place within this evolving waterfront district.

The development’s proximity to Marina Bay Sands, Marina Bay Link Mall, Suntec City, Raffles City, Satay by the Bay and an array of retail and dining options ensures that everything from world-class entertainment to everyday essentials is within easy reach. Nearby healthcare amenities, including medical and dental clinics, add an extra layer of convenience and reassurance.

With a walkable, well-connected neighbourhood, residents can reclaim time once spent in transit – whether for leisure, socialising, or quiet moments off-duty. The design encourages sustainable, car-lite living, making cycling or walking the natural choice in a neighbourhood built for the future.

One Marina Gardens is part of a broader vision by URA to create a pedestrian-friendly, car-lite community. Designed with walkability and connectivity in mind, the neighbourhood will feature shaded sidewalks, dedicated cycling paths and an underground pedestrian link, ensuring seamless movement while promoting eco-conscious urban living.

Affordable luxury within reach

As Marina South takes shape as one of Singapore’s most anticipated districts, One Marina Gardens presents a compelling investment opportunity for both homeowners and investors. Its proximity to major business and commercial hubs positions it as an attractive option for professionals seeking convenience – suggesting potential rental demand and long-term value.

With prices averaging $2,850 to $2,900 psf or starting at $1.16 million for a one-bedroom unit, the development offers a unique balance of luxury and accessibility, especially when compared to residences in the Marina Bay area. As the Greater Southern Waterfront master plan unfolds, new infrastructure and amenities are likely to enhance property values, reinforcing the area’s investment potential.

As Marina South further develops into a dynamic, mixed-use district, residents will find themselves at the heart of a flourishing neighbourhood, where growth and transformation may support long-term value.

Slated for completion in the third quarter of 2028, One Marina Gardens is more than a residence; it is a statement of modern city living, offering a rare opportunity to be part of an emerging landmark district.

For more information about One Marina Gardens, visit the Kingsford Marina Development website or call 9655-3808 or 8371-2006. The One Marina Gardens showflat is located along Marina Gardens Drive, next to Gardens by the Bay MRT Exit 2.

data.title: Retail investors may be able to tap private market assets under proposed framework
data.description:
SINGAPORE - Retail investors in Singapore could soon be able to put money into private market investment funds under a new framework that the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) is looking to introduce.

The framework, termed the long-term investment fund (LIF), could potentially cover assets such as private equity, which are shares of unlisted companies; and private credit from non-bank lenders. 

The MAS is seeking public feedback on the proposed move, which it said is spurred by growing interest from retail investors in tapping private markets.

There is also more interest from experienced industry players to offer such products to retail investors. 

“MAS has received feedback from market players in Singapore that there is potential for growth in this space given interest from retail investors to include private market investments as part of their diversified investment portfolios,” said the regulator in its consultation paper released on March 27.

Currently, retail investors can primarily buy funds that invest in public markets, such as listed shares and bonds. They have limited access to private market investments which are available to them only through authorised retail funds that are allowed to invest in certain alternative assets, such as unlisted shares; as well as real estate investment trusts (Reits) and unlisted property funds.

Retail investors can also invest in retail bonds backed by private equity, which are currently available in the market.

Investments such as private equity and private credit have traditionally been available only to accredited investors such as wealthy individuals, and institutional investors such as pension funds.

This is because these investments carry higher risks and tend to be long-term.

Retail investors also have different needs from institutional and accredited investors when it comes to private market investments.

Ms Long Pee Hua, partner at Allen & Gledhill, told The Straits Times that the release of the consultation is timely as part of the steps to revitalise Singapore’s equity markets.

“We will be closely reviewing the consultation paper and will submit our feedback to MAS,” said Ms Long.

Mr David Gerald, president and chief executive of Securities Investors Association (Singapore), said that private markets offer unique investment opportunities beyond traditional stocks and bonds, potentially boosting portfolio diversification and risk management.

For instance, investors can gain exposure to promising early-stage companies or projects that might not be readily available through traditional avenues, he said.

Private markets often offer higher potential returns than public markets, but with increased risk, said Mr Gerald, noting that private market investments are typically less liquid than public market investments, making it difficult to quickly sell assets.

“Retail investors might lack the experience and knowledge needed to evaluate and manage private market investments effectively,” he added.

The MAS outlined two possible structures that could be set up under the new framework to cater to different investing preferences: a direct fund and a long-term investment fund-of-funds.

A direct fund allows investors to buy into a specific private market asset directly. But it will not include property, as the MAS noted that retail investors can already access such assets via the Reits.

A long-term investment fund-of-funds, on the other hand, mainly invests in other private market investment funds which, in turn, may cover a variety of assets including real estate.

The range of assets that should be allowed under each structure is up for public feedback, according to the MAS paper.

data.title: STI hits new high on Thursday; closes up 0.5% amid mixed regional trading
data.description:
SINGAPORE – Local shares hit a new high in trading on March 27 before closing in the black, thanks to solid gains across most sectors.

The positive mood evident all week remained intact to send the Straits Times Index (STI) to as high as 3,991 points in the morning before closing at 3,981.57, up 0.5 per cent or 17.86 points for the day.

Gainers outstripped losers 274 to 218 on trade of one billion securities worth $1.4 billion.

The gains here came in spite of another downbeat session on Wall Street overnight, as tariff fears rattled investors, sending the Nasdaq down 2 per cent while the S&P 500 lost 1 per cent and the Dow Industrials dipped 0.5 per cent.

Regional indexes were mixed. Hong Kong’s Hang Seng rose 0.4 per cent and Malaysian stocks added 1.2 per cent but the Nikkei 225 in Tokyo lost 0.6 per cent, the Kospi in Seoul fell 1.4 per cent and Australia’s ASX200 retreated 0.4 per cent to snap a five-day winning streak

On the home front, the STI’s top gainer was DFI Retail Group, up 2.1 per cent to US$2.44, while Yangzijiang Shipbuilding was the biggest decliner, down 0.8 per cent to $2.40.

The banks rose: DBS added 0.4 per cent to $46.58; OCBC was up 0.8 per cent to $17.38; and UOB advanced 1.1 per cent to $38.26.

ST Engineering and Sembcorp Industries have led gains in the first quarter, with analysts raising their 12-month consensus price targets for both counters, noted Singapore Exchange market strategist Geoff Howie.

He added that industrials booked the most net institutional inflow in March, bucking the broader net outflows.

Industrials are benefiting from China’s efforts to strengthen regional supply chains, while Asean policymakers continue to bolster economic integration and movement of capital goods within the region.

Mr Howie noted: “This has put industrial value chains clearly in the frame, impacting engineering, construction, manufacturing and transportation-related industries.” THE BUSINESS TIMES
